content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 19, 1990


Barbara J. Culver
City Clerk
City of Palos Verdes Estates
P.O. Box 1086
Palos Verdes Estates, CA  90274-0283

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-90-140

Dear Ms. Culver:

     You have requested advice regarding your duties as city clerk
with respect to the application of the mass mailing provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹  This letter confirms
telephone advice provided to you on March 7, 1990.

     This letter presents an issue which we have not previously
considered.  We have provided advice which we believe accurately
interprets a recently adopted regulation.  We recognize that this
is conservative advice on a difficult question, therefore, we
intend to provide this letter to the Commission for review at a
future meeting.

QUESTIONS

     1.  May the city newsletter contain a list of candidates for
election in the city general election where one of the candidates
is the current mayor of the city?

     2.  May the city newsletter reprint a page from the sample
ballot which lists the candidates?




___________________
1  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-90-140
Page 2

CONCLUSION

1. If the newsletter is published completely independently
of the mayor, city council, and other elected officers affiliated
with the city, it may contain the list of candidates, including
the mayor's name. The mayor's name must not be singled out for
attention by type size, typeface or type color.

2. A page from the sample ballot, showing the candidates'
names, could not be reprinted in the newsletter.

FACTS

According to the facts which you have provided, the City of
Palos Verdes Estates is having its general election in April 1990.
You are the appointed city clerk and write the newsletter,
which is published quarterly to inform city residents regarding
city issues and events. The edition which precedes the general
election by one week will remind city residents to vote, lists polling
places, election time, and items on the ballot, such as measures
and candidates. This year, one of the candidates on the ballot is
the current mayor of the city. You wish to know if the mayor's
name may be included in the list of candidates without violating
the mass mailing provision of the Act. You have suggested either
a listing of the candidates be printed or that a page from the
sample ballot which lists the candidates be reprinted.

ANALYSIS

Section 89001 of the Act provides that "no newsletter or
other mass mailing shall be sent at public expense." A mass mail-
ing is defined as two hundred or more substantially similar pieces
of mail. (Section 82041.5.) Regulation 189012 (copy enclosed)
clarifies that the purpose of Section 89001 is to prevent elected
officers from using public funds to send newsletters and other
mass mailings at public expense. It provides that a mass mailing
may not be sent at public expense if it contains the name, office,
photograph or any other reference to an elected officer affiliated
with the agency producing or sending the mailing and is prepared
or sent in cooperation, coordination or concert with
the elected officer. (Regulation 18901(a).)

If the elected officers affiliated with the agency are not
involved in the production or sending of the mailing, so that the

2 A revised version of Regulation 18901 was adopted by the Com-
mission on December 13, 1989. A statutorily required public com-
ment period expired January 17, 1990. Although the revised ver-
sion of the regulation is still in the rulemaking process, it
represents the policy of the Commission. Therefore, we are advis-
ing agencies that they may rely on it. The advice contained in
this letter is based upon the revised version of Regulation 18901.

Our File No. A-90-140
Page 3

items are prepared and sent completely independently of the
elected officers, the items sent may include names of the
elected officers in a manner which does not "feature" them.
(Regulation 18901(a)(2)(A).) In this situation, names of or
references to the elected officers could be included in the text,
provided that they are not singled out by manner of display of the
name of office in the layout, such as by headline captions, type
size, typeface, or type color. (Regulation 18901(c)(2).)

A mailing is prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation,
coordination or concert with an elected officer if the elected
officers affiliated with an agency are in any way involved with
the production or distribution of the items sent. This means, for
example, that the elected officers affiliated with the agency may
not review the mailing or make suggestions for articles to be
included. (Stone Advice Letter, 89-670, copy enclosed.)

An elected officer is affiliated with an agency if he or she
is a member, officer, or employee of the agency, or of a subsidiary
thereof, or has supervisory control over the agency, or appoints
one or more members of the agency. (Regulation 18901(c)(1).) The
mayor and the city council and any other elected officials
are included within the definition of elected officers who are
affiliated with the city.

Therefore, the names of the mayor, the city councilmembers,
or other elected officers affiliated with the city may not be
included in the city newsletter if it is produced in cooperation,
consultation, coordination or concert with them, including such
acts as reviewing the newsletter or making suggestions for
articles. If the newsletter is prepared and sent completely
independently of the elected officers, it may include their names
or offices in the text, provided they are not "featured."

With respect to your situation, if the pre-election edition
of the newsletter is published completely independently of the
mayor, the city council, and any other elected officers affiliated
with the city, the newsletter may contain the list of candidates,
including the mayor's name. The mayor's name must not be singled
out for attention by type size, typeface or type color.

You have also suggested the possibility of including in the
newsletter a reprint of the candidate page from the sample ballot.
When a jurisdiction mails sample ballots to registered voters as
part of the election process, the mailing does not violate the
mass mailing restrictions, because it is mailed pursuant to an
exception. That exception permits an elected officer's name to be
included in an item sent, if the item is sent as required by law
and the use of the elected officer's name is necessary in the
mailing. (Regulation 18901 (f)(6).) However, reprinting all or a
portion of the sample ballot in the city's newsletter is not
required by law. Therefore, the exception for legally-required
mailings does not apply.

Our File No. A-90-140
Page 4

     Again, we recognize that this is a conservative
interpretation of Regulation 18901, and we intend to provide this
letter to the Commission for review at a future meeting.

     If you have any questions regarding the above, you may
contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Kathryn E. Donovan
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]

                                        By: Margaret W. Ellison
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

KED:MWE:aa

Enclosures
